proposals.title: Robust Learning of Subgaussian Distributions via Hamiltonian Dynamics and Filtering
proposals.motivation: Robust mean and covariance estimation under adversarial corruption is a cornerstone of reliable machine learning and statistics. Pre-2026, many breakthrough algorithms achieving information-theoretically optimal error rates rely critically on the assumption that the uncorrupted data is Gaussian. This assumption is often violated in practice, where data may only be subgaussian, limiting the applicability of these robust estimators in real-world settings.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by leveraging the dynamical structure and rapid mixing properties of quantum-inspired or classical Markov chains, we can design computationally efficient filtering algorithms that achieve near-optimal robust estimation error for arbitrary subgaussian distributions.
proposals.method: We will adapt the framework of learning from Hamiltonian dynamics and Markov random fields, as in Moitra's recent work, to the robust setting. The core technical approach is to model the uncorrupted subgaussian distribution as the stationary distribution of a rapidly mixing Hamiltonian or Markov process. We will then design a spectral or moment-matching filter that operates on the evolved state or its empirical correlations, using the dynamical constraints to certify the subgaussian moments of the good data and isolate corruptions. This connects high-temperature Dobrushin conditions (ensuring fast mixing) to the stability of sample moments.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first computationally efficient, near-optimal robust estimators for mean and covariance under the minimal assumption of subgaussianity, bridging a significant gap between theory and practice. It will also establish a novel connection between robust statistics and the analysis of mixing times in classical and quantum systems.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in moving beyond the Gaussianity barrier in robust estimation by using dynamical system perspectives as a certifiable proxy for distributional assumptions, a synthesis not present in pre-2026 robust statistics or Hamiltonian learning literature.

proposals.title: Polynomial-Time Covariance Estimation for High-Temperature Quantum Gibbs States via Rapidly Mixing Lindblad Dynamics
proposals.motivation: Estimating the covariance matrix of a quantum Gibbs state is fundamental for learning quantum many-body systems and certifying correlations. Pre-2026 algorithms, such as those based on the Gentle Measurement Lemma, achieve quasipolynomial runtime in the inverse precision 1/ε, which is prohibitively expensive for high-precision tasks. A fully polynomial-time algorithm would dramatically improve the efficiency of learning and verifying properties of quantum states relevant to quantum simulation and Hamiltonian learning.
proposals.hypothesis: By leveraging the rapid mixing properties of high-temperature quantum Markov chains (Lindblad dynamics) and their connection to conditional mutual information decay, one can design a sampling-based estimator that achieves a covariance estimation error ε in time polynomial in 1/ε and system size.
proposals.method: We will construct a dissipative Lindblad dynamics whose fixed point is the target Gibbs state, building on the Dobrushin-type condition for rapid mixing established in Moitra's prior work. The covariance will be estimated by measuring time-evolved observables under this dynamics, using the evolution to generate approximate, independent samples. The polynomial runtime will be proven by bounding the mixing time in terms of the system parameters and ε, and integrating this with a robust mean estimation framework over the trajectory data, bypassing the need for costly state tomography.
proposals.contribution: This work would provide the first polynomial-time algorithm for covariance estimation of high-temperature quantum Gibbs states, directly improving the runtime dependency from quasipolynomial to polynomial in 1/ε. It would bridge the theory of quantum Markov chain mixing with practical learning algorithms, offering a new tool for efficient property testing and structure learning of quantum systems.
proposals.novelty: The non-obvious step is to use the engineered, rapidly mixing Lindblad dynamics not just for state preparation, but as an integral part of a sampling-based estimator, transforming a mixing time guarantee into a concrete computational advantage for a fundamental learning task where quasipolynomial time was the known barrier.

proposals.title: Closing the √2 Gap: Optimal Constants for Robust Mean Estimation via Hamiltonian-Based Filtering
proposals.motivation: Robust mean estimation under adversarial contamination is a fundamental problem in high-dimensional statistics and learning. Pre-2026 work established an information-theoretic lower bound of Θ(√(ε log(1/ε))) for error, but the best-known polynomial-time algorithms, like the filtering approach, incurred an extra √2 factor in the leading constant. This gap represents a precise, unresolved tension between statistical optimality and computational efficiency in a canonical setting.
proposals.hypothesis: The √2 factor is not inherent to polynomial-time computation; it can be eliminated by a novel algorithmic framework that more tightly couples the primal estimation problem with a dual certification procedure, potentially leveraging spectral or semidefinite programming structures.
proposals.method: We will formulate robust mean estimation as finding a state (density matrix) maximizing overlap with a Hamiltonian constructed from the empirical data and a carefully chosen constraint term, inspired by recent methods for learning Hamiltonians from dynamics. The core technical approach is to design a primal-dual meta-algorithm where the primal step performs a gentle, iterative Hamiltonian-based evolution to filter outliers, and the dual step certifies optimality via a stability condition akin to a Dobrushin-type criterion for the resulting quantum-inspired Markov chain. This will unify and extend tools from Moitra's recent work on structure learning from dynamics and rapid mixing of quantum Markov chains.
proposals.contribution: This work would deliver the first polynomial-time algorithm for robust mean estimation achieving the optimal leading error constant, settling a well-known open problem. It would introduce a new Hamiltonian-based perspective to robust statistics, potentially leading to optimal constants for other related estimation tasks.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in synthesizing concepts from quantum Markov chains and Hamiltonian learning—domains where Moitra has recently developed techniques—to attack a purely classical statistical optimality gap, a connection not present in prior filtering or SDP-based robust estimation literature.

proposals.title: Robust Gaussian Estimation via Sum-of-Squares: A Convex Relaxation Framework for Adversarial Contamination
proposals.motivation: Robustly estimating a Gaussian distribution from corrupted samples is a fundamental problem in statistics and learning, with applications from high-dimensional data analysis to algorithmic fairness. Pre-2026, efficient algorithms achieving optimal error rates under strong contamination models (e.g., the Huber contamination model) remained elusive for high-dimensional Gaussians, especially for parameter recovery beyond mean estimation. Existing convex relaxations often fail to capture the complex structure of the problem.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the Sum-of-Squares (SoS) hierarchy can provide a principled, convex programming framework for robust Gaussian estimation that achieves information-theoretically optimal error rates in polynomial time, even for covariance estimation and in the presence of a constant fraction of adversarial outliers.
proposals.method: We will formulate robust mean and covariance estimation as a feasibility problem over pseudo-distributions consistent with the observed, corrupted samples. The core technical approach is to design a high-degree SoS relaxation that certifies the existence of a large, clean subset of the data conforming to a Gaussian model. This involves constructing SoS proofs of key statistical facts (e.g., concentration of empirical moments for inliers) within the relaxation. We will then develop a rounding algorithm, potentially inspired by recent work on SoS for learning mixtures, to extract the estimated parameters from the pseudo-expectation.
proposals.contribution: This work would provide the first polynomial-time, SoS-based algorithm for optimal robust estimation of high-dimensional Gaussians, bridging the gap between statistical rates and computational efficiency. It would also advance the methodological toolkit for robust statistics by demonstrating how SoS can systematically enforce complex statistical constraints in convex programs.
proposals.novelty: While SoS has been applied to robust mean estimation for bounded-covariance distributions, its application to the full Gaussian estimation problem (mean and covariance) under strong contamination is novel and non-trivial, requiring new SoS-certifiable arguments about higher-order moment structures and their resilience to corruption.

proposals.title: Robust Structure Learning from Incomplete Observations via Hamiltonian Dynamics and Matrix Completion
proposals.motivation: Learning the structure of complex systems from observational data is a core challenge in machine learning and statistical physics. In many real-world settings, such as biological sensing or quantum state tomography, data is both incomplete (with many missing entries) and potentially corrupted by outliers. Pre-2026 methods for robust estimation and matrix completion are largely decoupled, leaving a gap for principled learning from such challenging data.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by integrating robust statistical estimators with low-rank matrix completion within a dynamical systems framework, we can provably learn the underlying structure (e.g., Hamiltonian or Markov field) even when a significant fraction of observations are missing and the remainder are subject to adversarial corruption.
proposals.method: We will leverage Moitra's recent work on learning Hamiltonians from real-time evolution and higher-order Markov fields from dynamics. The approach will formulate the incomplete, corrupted observation problem as learning a low-rank interaction matrix from subsampled and noisy time-evolution data. We will develop an alternating minimization algorithm that robustly imputes missing entries using a trimmed loss function (e.g., trimmed L2) while simultaneously enforcing low-rank structure via nuclear norm regularization. Theoretical analysis will draw upon the Dobrushin condition framework to ensure rapid mixing and stable recovery.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a unified algorithmic framework for robust learning from incomplete dynamical data, with provable recovery guarantees. It will extend the applicability of Hamiltonian and Markov field learning to highly imperfect data regimes common in experimental sciences. The theoretical results will bridge concepts from robust statistics, matrix completion, and dynamical systems.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the joint handling of adversarial corruptions and systematic missingness within a single dynamical learning framework, moving beyond prior work that typically addresses only one of these imperfections. The integration of a 'trim-and-complete' procedure with the analysis of quantum or classical Markov chain dynamics is a non-obvious synthesis.

proposals.title: Learning High-Dimensional Distributions and Hamiltonians with Sample Complexity Near the Intrinsic Dimension
proposals.motivation: In many scientific domains, such as quantum simulation and complex dynamical systems, data is extremely expensive to generate, limiting the number of samples to a scale comparable to the intrinsic dimension of the underlying model. Pre-2026 learning theory often requires polynomially many samples in the dimension, which is infeasible in these settings. Bridging this gap is critical for applying machine learning to cutting-edge physics and biology experiments.
proposals.hypothesis: By leveraging structural assumptions like locality in Hamiltonians or conditional independence in high-temperature Gibbs states, one can design algorithms that learn distributions and generators from a number of samples scaling only with the intrinsic model complexity, bypassing polynomial dependencies on the ambient dimension.
proposals.method: We will extend the Hamiltonian learning framework from real-time evolution (Moitra, 2025) to the ultra-low sample regime by integrating Dobrushin-type conditions for rapid mixing to enable robust estimation from short trajectories. For learning higher-order Markov Random Fields from dynamics, we will develop new spectral or moment methods that exploit the dynamical data to reduce sample complexity, connecting to the 'bypassing the noisy parity barrier' approach. Core techniques will include analyzing the concentration of conditional mutual information estimators and using Lieb-Robinson bounds to constrain learning to local patches.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first sample-efficient learning guarantees for quantum and classical spin systems in the regime where samples are scarce. It will provide a rigorous theoretical foundation for data-driven scientific discovery in expensive experimental settings. The algorithms will demonstrate a concrete path to learning complex interactions from real-time evolution data without exponential or heavy polynomial overhead.
proposals.novelty: Existing work focuses on polynomial sample complexities; achieving near-intrinsic-dimension scaling by jointly exploiting dynamical data and high-temperature structural properties is a significant and non-obvious leap. The synthesis of rapid mixing analysis with Hamiltonian learning in this low-sample context is unexplored.

proposals.title: Robust Structure Learning of Local Hamiltonians from Adversarially Perturbed Dynamics
proposals.motivation: Recent breakthroughs in learning Hamiltonians from quantum dynamics, including Moitra's own work, assume i.i.d. or benign noise. However, realistic quantum devices and adversarial environments can introduce structured, worst-case perturbations. Developing algorithms robust to such noise is critical for reliable quantum system identification and benchmarking in noisy intermediate-scale quantum (NISQ) era devices.
proposals.hypothesis: That the intrinsic locality and geometric constraints of many-body quantum systems can be leveraged to design efficient algorithms that learn Hamiltonian structure even when the observed time-evolution data is corrupted by bounded adversarial noise, circumventing the fragility of methods designed for stochastic noise.
proposals.method: We will adapt the framework of learning from real-time evolution, but replace the standard least-squares loss with a robust objective function, such as a truncated or Huber loss, resilient to outliers. The core technical challenge is to analyze the stability of the tensor decomposition or moment-matching procedures used in Hamiltonian identification under an ℓ∞-bounded adversarial perturbation model. We will employ tools from robust statistics and high-dimensional probability to derive new concentration results for corrupted quantum expectation values.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first provably robust algorithms for Hamiltonian learning under adversarial noise, bridging quantum learning theory with classical robust estimation. It will establish new sample complexity bounds and pave the way for more fault-tolerant quantum characterization protocols.
proposals.novelty: While robust classical learning is well-studied, applying these principles to the quantum dynamical setting—where data comes from inherently noisy quantum measurements and the underlying model has a specific Lie algebraic structure—is a novel and non-trivial direction.

proposals.title: Sharp Detection Thresholds and Efficient Algorithms in the Spiked Wishart Model with p/n → c
proposals.motivation: Understanding the fundamental limits of detecting low-rank structure in high-dimensional covariance matrices is a central problem in statistics and learning. The spiked Wishart model in the proportional asymptotic regime, where the dimension p and sample size n grow with p/n → c > 0, is a canonical setting for studying such phase transitions. Pre-2026 work has established precise thresholds for the classical PCA estimator, but the algorithmic landscape—especially for computationally efficient methods below the PCA threshold—remains less explored, with connections to statistical physics and inference in graphical models.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that for the spiked Wishart model with a rank-one spike, there exists a sharp computational-statistical gap: detection is information-theoretically possible above the BBP transition (λ > 1/√c), but computationally hard for a broad class of polynomial-time algorithms below a conjectured algorithmic threshold (λ < c^{1/4}), mirroring phenomena in the spiked Wigner model.
proposals.method: We will employ a combination of average-case reduction techniques and spectral analysis of non-backtracking operators or tensorized matrices, inspired by methods for the spiked Wigner model. The core technical approach involves analyzing the landscape of the associated likelihood or a suitable spectral statistic in the high-dimensional limit, potentially leveraging tools from random matrix theory and the cavity method to characterize the performance of approximate message passing (AMP) algorithms. We will also explore connections to learning higher-order Markov Random Fields from dynamics, as the Wishart model arises from covariance estimation of Gaussian vectors.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous characterization of the computational detection threshold for the spiked Wishart model, establishing a sharp phase transition for efficient algorithms. It will contribute new algorithmic insights, potentially identifying optimal spectral methods that work all the way down to the conjectured computational limit, thereby bridging the gap between statistical theory and practical inference in high dimensions.
proposals.novelty: While the information-theoretic threshold is known, a rigorous computational lower bound for the Wishart model in the proportional regime, and the design of optimal efficient algorithms achieving it, would be a significant advance beyond the well-understood Wigner case and prior work focused primarily on the PCA limit.

proposals.title: Robustness of Hamiltonian Learning and State Reconstruction Under Non-Gaussian Perturbations
proposals.motivation: A foundational assumption in many quantum learning and classical distribution learning algorithms is that perturbations or noise are Gaussian, enabling clean analysis via concentration of measure. In practice, noise can be structured, bounded, or even adversarial. Moitra's recent work on learning Hamiltonians from real-time evolution and higher-order Markov fields from dynamics provides a perfect testbed to relax this assumption and establish more realistic robustness guarantees.
proposals.hypothesis: The algorithmic frameworks for learning Hamiltonians and Markov random fields from dynamics can be made robust to a broader class of perturbation models, including uniform and adversarial bounded noise, without a catastrophic loss in sample complexity or computational efficiency.
proposals.method: We will adapt the spectral and tensor methods from Moitra's recent papers to analyze stability under non-Gaussian noise. For adversarial bounded perturbations, we will employ matrix and tensor concentration inequalities valid for all distributions supported on a norm-bounded set. For uniform noise, we will leverage anti-concentration properties. The analysis will focus on key algorithmic steps like spectral decomposition of correlation matrices and tensor recovery, deriving new perturbation bounds under these noise models.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first robustness guarantees for Hamiltonian and MRF learning under realistic, non-Gaussian noise, significantly broadening their applicability. It will also contribute new technical tools for stability analysis in high-dimensional learning problems beyond the standard Gaussian or sub-Gaussian settings.
proposals.novelty: While robustness to Gaussian noise is well-studied, a systematic analysis for Hamiltonian learning algorithms under adversarial bounded or uniform noise models is largely unexplored, requiring new analytical techniques beyond standard concentration.

proposals.title: Learning Correlated Tensor Decompositions from Hamiltonian Dynamics
proposals.motivation: Tensor decomposition is a fundamental tool for learning latent variable models, but standard methods assume components are independent. In physical systems, such as quantum many-body states or higher-order Markov random fields, components exhibit complex correlations due to underlying dynamics or interactions. Pre-2026 work on learning Hamiltonians from evolution and higher-order MRFs from dynamics highlights the need for decomposition methods that can handle these correlations to accurately recover system structure.
proposals.hypothesis: The dynamical evolution generated by a local Hamiltonian imposes a specific correlation structure on tensor components of observables, which can be exploited to develop statistically efficient and computationally tractable learning algorithms for correlated tensor decompositions.
proposals.method: We will model correlations via a graphical model or a quantum Markov network on the component indices, informed by the Hamiltonian's interaction graph. The approach will extend the framework of 'Structure Learning of Hamiltonians from Real-Time Evolution' by formulating the recovery of correlated components as a constrained tensor factorization problem. We will develop algorithms using alternating minimization with a proximal step to enforce the correlation constraints, leveraging tools from the analysis of quantum Markov chains (e.g., Dobrushin conditions) to ensure convergence and sample efficiency.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic framework for learning tensor models with correlated components, directly applicable to learning interacting physical systems from time-series data. It will extend the algorithmic toolkit for structure learning beyond the independent-component or low-temperature regimes, enabling new applications in quantum simulation and complex system inference.
proposals.novelty: While tensor decomposition and Hamiltonian learning are established, combining them to explicitly model and learn correlated component structures from dynamics is a novel direction. This moves beyond the standard 'bypassing the noisy parity barrier' setting by addressing correlations inherent in the components themselves, not just in the observed data.

proposals.title: Robust Independence Lemmas for Learning Non-Gaussian and Quantum Distributions
proposals.motivation: Robust linear independence lemmas have proven powerful for learning multi-view mixture models and Gaussian distributions, but many real-world learning problems involve non-Gaussian, heavy-tailed, or quantum data. The core challenge is extending these robustness guarantees to broader settings where perturbation structures are more complex, such as in learning from dynamical evolution or quantum states. This would bridge a gap between classical algorithmic stability results and modern learning tasks in high-dimensional statistics and quantum information.
proposals.hypothesis: The robust linear independence lemma can be generalized to handle perturbations arising from non-linear dynamics and quantum measurements, enabling efficient parameter recovery for a wider class of latent variable models and Hamiltonian learning problems.
proposals.method: We will first formalize a generalized robust independence condition for vectors perturbed by non-Gaussian noise and by quantum measurement operators. This will involve analyzing the perturbation structure in problems like learning higher-order Markov Random Fields from dynamics and structure learning of Hamiltonians from real-time evolution. We will then develop and analyze spectral or tensor decomposition algorithms that leverage this condition, proving sample complexity bounds for parameter estimation in these models under our generalized lemma.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a unified theoretical tool for robust learning in non-Gaussian and quantum settings, directly applicable to Moitra's recent lines of work on learning from dynamics and Hamiltonian structure learning. It will yield new, efficient algorithms with provable guarantees for learning complex distributions beyond the reach of current robust independence techniques.
proposals.novelty: While robust independence is established for linear perturbations in classical statistics, its application to the non-linear perturbations of dynamical systems and the non-commutative nature of quantum observables is unexplored and non-trivial, requiring a fresh analytical framework.

proposals.title: Robust Topic Recovery via Adversarial Mixture Models: A Spectral and Hamiltonian Dynamics Approach
proposals.motivation: Topic modeling algorithms, like LDA, typically assume document-topic mixtures are drawn from a simple prior (e.g., Dirichlet), which can fail catastrophically under adversarial or worst-case data. This vulnerability limits the reliability of learned models in security-critical applications or when analyzing curated or manipulated corpora. A framework for provable recovery under adversarial mixtures would significantly strengthen the foundations of unsupervised learning from text.
proposals.hypothesis: By leveraging spectral methods on higher-order co-occurrence statistics derived from document dynamics, and by modeling the data generation as an evolution under a local Hamiltonian, one can provably recover the underlying topic model even when an adversary chooses the document-topic mixtures.
proposals.method: We will extend the method of moments framework for learning latent variable models to an adversarial setting. Building on Moitra's recent work on learning Hamiltonians from real-time evolution and higher-order Markov Random Fields from dynamics, we will model the generation of words within a document as a local quantum or classical dynamical process. The key algorithmic step is to construct robust tensor decompositions of appropriately defined 'time-evolved' co-occurrence tensors, whose spectral properties are guaranteed by the locality and rapid mixing of the underlying dynamics, even under adversarial mixture weights. We will employ the Dobrushin condition analysis to control error propagation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first provable guarantees for topic model recovery under adversarial mixtures, bridging robust statistics and spectral learning. It will introduce new techniques from Hamiltonian learning and analysis of quantum Markov chains into the adversarial machine learning toolkit, demonstrating their utility for classical robustness problems.
proposals.novelty: The non-obvious connection is the use of dynamical systems and Hamiltonian evolution to generate robust spectral signatures that bypass the need for benign probabilistic assumptions on the mixture weights, moving beyond the standard i.i.d. or well-conditioned random design paradigms.

proposals.title: Query-Oblivious Learning: Provable Recovery Against Adaptive Adversaries via Information-Limited Dynamics
proposals.motivation: Modern learning algorithms interact with data over multiple adaptive queries, creating a vulnerability where an adversary can corrupt future data based on observed queries or intermediate outputs. Pre-2026 work in robust statistics often assumes a static or oblivious adversary, while differential privacy focuses on privacy, not necessarily robustness. A theoretical framework for recovery against a query-aware, adaptive adversary is urgently needed for secure learning in interactive settings like active learning or quantum state tomography.
proposals.hypothesis: By rigorously limiting the information leaked by an algorithm's execution path through techniques from differential privacy and online learning, one can design algorithms that provably recover underlying parameters (e.g., a distribution, Hamiltonian, or MRF) even against an adaptive adversary whose corruptions are strategically correlated with the algorithm's queries.
proposals.method: We will model the interaction as a multi-round game, framing the algorithm's queries as a sequence of measurements on a system (classical or quantum). The core technical approach is to integrate differential privacy mechanisms (e.g., the exponential mechanism, sparse vector technique) to randomize and bound the information in each query. We will analyze this using tools from conditional mutual information and Dobrushin-type conditions, inspired by Moitra's work on quantum Markov chains, to control the adversary's ability to infer and exploit the algorithm's state. Recovery guarantees will be derived by combining these information-limited dynamics with robust statistics for the final aggregated data.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formal framework quantifying the trade-off between query complexity, transparency, and robustness in adversarial learning. It will yield new, provably robust algorithms for structure learning of Hamiltonians from real-time evolution and learning higher-order Markov random fields, where the adversary can corrupt the observed dynamics. The results will bridge differential privacy, online learning, and robust statistics in a novel way.
proposals.novelty: Existing robust learning theory largely ignores the adaptive, query-aware adversary, while differential privacy does not directly guarantee parameter recovery under such attacks. The novelty lies in using information-limiting dynamics, inspired by analysis of quantum systems, to explicitly control and leverage the adversary's strategic correlation for provable recovery.

proposals.title: Robust Dictionary Learning via Tensor Decomposition of Higher-Order Cumulants
proposals.motivation: Standard dictionary learning algorithms, which rely on second-order statistics, are highly vulnerable to adversarial or strongly correlated noise, limiting their application in real-world, noisy data settings. Pre-2026 work has shown that higher-order moments can isolate signal from Gaussian noise, but their potential for robustness against worst-case, non-Gaussian perturbations is underexplored. This project addresses a critical gap in building reliable, provable learning systems for corrupted data.
proposals.hypothesis: That adversarial noise, while capable of arbitrarily corrupting covariance matrices, has a bounded and structured effect on third- and fourth-order empirical cumulants, enabling stable recovery of dictionary atoms via robust tensor decomposition methods.
proposals.method: We will formulate the noisy observation model and derive the analytical form of its higher-order cumulant tensors. The core algorithm will involve constructing these empirical cumulants from data and applying a robust tensor power method or alternating least squares, drawing on pre-2026 tensor decomposition frameworks. We will integrate techniques from Moitra's recent work on learning from dynamics and Hamiltonian evolution to design initialization and convergence criteria that are stable under the adversarial noise model, providing end-to-end recovery guarantees.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first provably robust dictionary learning algorithm against a broad class of adversarial noise models, extending the reach of sparse coding to less curated data. It will also contribute a new theoretical framework linking the stability of tensor methods to the moment structure of adversarial perturbations, bridging dictionary learning and robust statistics.
proposals.novelty: The non-obvious insight is that adversarial noise, designed to defeat second-order methods, becomes statistically 'weaker' in higher-order tensor representations, allowing for decomposition where matrix methods fundamentally fail. This moves beyond prior uses of tensors for Gaussian noise to a new theory for worst-case robustness.

proposals.title: Robust Hamiltonian and Markov Field Learning from Dynamics with Adversarial and Corrupted Observations
proposals.motivation: Algorithms for learning Hamiltonians or Markov random fields from dynamical data, such as Moitra's recent work on learning from real-time evolution, typically assume well-behaved (e.g., Gaussian, independent) noise. Real-world quantum sensors and biological systems exhibit complex, correlated, or even adversarial noise, which can catastrophically break standard recovery guarantees. Developing robust methods is crucial for applying these powerful learning frameworks to practical experimental settings.
proposals.hypothesis: By leveraging the inherent geometric and information-theoretic structure of quantum Markov chains and higher-order Markov dynamics, one can design algorithms that tolerate a bounded fraction of adversarially corrupted observations or non-Gaussian, temporally correlated noise, while maintaining provable sample efficiency.
proposals.method: We will adapt the framework of robust statistics, specifically filtering techniques for heavy-tailed and adversarial corruption, to the dynamical learning setting. For Hamiltonian learning, we will integrate median-of-means estimators into the spectral or gradient-based recovery process, analyzing stability under the Dobrushin-type conditions from Moitra's prior work. For learning Markov random fields from dynamics, we will develop a robust variant of the conditional mutual information tests, using truncation or influence functions to limit the impact of corrupted trajectory segments.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first provably robust algorithms for learning physical models from dynamical data with adversarial noise, significantly expanding their applicability. It will establish new connections between robust statistics, quantum information theory (via conditional mutual information), and dynamical systems, yielding concrete recovery guarantees under realistic noise models.
proposals.novelty: While robust learning is studied in static settings, applying these principles to the sequential, correlated data generated by Hamiltonian evolution or Markovian dynamics is largely unexplored. The novelty lies in tailoring adversarial robustness guarantees to the specific structural constraints (e.g., locality, rapid mixing) inherent in these physical systems.

proposals.title: Joint Structure Learning and Signal Recovery via Adaptive Hierarchical Priors
proposals.motivation: Compressed sensing and sparse recovery often assume signals follow a known, simple prior (e.g., i.i.d. sparsity or a known Markov Random Field). In practice, the underlying structure of non-zero coefficients—such as clustering, hierarchies, or other complex dependencies—is unknown and non-random. Pre-2026 algorithms are brittle to model misspecification, limiting their deployment in scientific and machine learning applications where signal structure must be inferred from data.
proposals.hypothesis: A hierarchical Bayesian model with flexible, non-parametric priors can simultaneously learn the latent support structure and recover the signal from compressed measurements, achieving robust recovery guarantees even under approximate structure modeling.
proposals.method: We will develop a framework using the Indian Buffet Process (IBP) and structured spike-and-slab priors to model the support pattern without pre-specifying its dimensionality or exact form. Inference will be performed via scalable variational methods or MCMC, building on recent work in learning Hamiltonians from dynamics to treat the support structure as a latent variable evolving under a learned potential. Theoretical analysis will adapt techniques from high-temperature quantum Markov chains and Dobrushin conditions to prove rapid mixing of the inference algorithm and establish `approximate structure modeling' guarantees for recovery.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a unified algorithm for adaptive sparse recovery that does not require a known structural prior. It will extend theoretical recovery guarantees to the setting of learned, approximate models, bridging Bayesian nonparametrics and compressed sensing. The resulting system will be more practical for real-world applications like imaging and network inference where structure is complex and unknown.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work that assumes a known structural model, this approach jointly learns the structure from the compressed data itself. The theoretical framework for robustness under model misspecification, drawing connections to mixing times in statistical physics, is a novel direction for sparse recovery.

proposals.title: Learning Sparse Hamiltonians and Markov Random Fields with Structured Sparsity Patterns
proposals.motivation: Recent breakthroughs in learning Hamiltonians and higher-order Markov Random Fields (MRFs) from dynamics, including Moitra's own work, rely on the assumption that non-zero coefficients are uniformly random. This is unrealistic for many physical and biological systems, where interactions often follow structured patterns (e.g., geometric locality, hierarchical organization, or specific symmetries). Extending recovery guarantees to such structured sparsity is crucial for applying these algorithms to real-world data.
proposals.hypothesis: By explicitly modeling the underlying structure of the sparsity pattern (e.g., via graphical constraints or low-dimensional embeddings), we can develop efficient algorithms with provable sample complexity guarantees that are significantly better than those for worst-case, unstructured sparsity.
proposals.method: We will adapt the framework of learning from real-time evolution and dynamics. First, we will define a class of structured sparsity models (e.g., k-local Hamiltonians on a known lattice, or MRFs with bounded treewidth or community structure). We will then modify existing L1-regularized maximum likelihood estimators to incorporate structural priors, potentially using group lasso or structured sparse regularizers. The analysis will leverage the Dobrushin-type conditions from Moitra's recent work to control the propagation of information and prove rapid mixing, enabling efficient recovery even when the non-zero coefficients are not uniformly distributed.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first provable guarantees for learning interacting systems with non-random, structured interactions from dynamical data. It will bridge the gap between theoretical compressed sensing models and practical applications in quantum simulation and network inference. The algorithms will be validated on synthetic data from structured Ising models and local quantum spin chains.
proposals.novelty: While structured sparsity is studied in classical statistics, its integration into the Hamiltonian/MRF learning-from-dynamics framework is novel. The key challenge is to maintain algorithmic efficiency and provable guarantees when the sparsity pattern is constrained, not just random, which requires new analysis of the underlying dynamical system's properties.

proposals.title: Parameterized Complexity of Learning Sparse Hamiltonians: Breaking or Establishing the Polynomial k-Barrier
proposals.motivation: Learning the Hamiltonian of a quantum system from its dynamics is a central task in quantum machine learning and quantum simulation. For sparse k-local Hamiltonians, state-of-the-art algorithms (pre-2026) exhibit polynomial dependence on the sparsity parameter k, which becomes prohibitive for systems with high-order interactions. Determining if this dependence is fundamental or an artifact of current methods is critical for understanding the intrinsic difficulty of learning complex quantum systems and for guiding algorithm development.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the polynomial dependence on the sparsity parameter k is not intrinsic, and that an algorithm with sub-polynomial (e.g., logarithmic or poly-logarithmic) dependence on k can be constructed for learning from real-time evolution under standard physical assumptions.
proposals.method: We will adapt the recent framework of learning Hamiltonians from real-time evolution (Moitra et al., pre-2026) to a parameterized complexity analysis. The approach will leverage the connection between Hamiltonian learning and learning higher-order Markov Random Fields from dynamics, exploring whether the 'noisy parity barrier' can be bypassed in the quantum setting via implicit representations. We will develop adaptive, evolution-time-dependent measurement strategies and employ advanced matrix sketching techniques to avoid explicit enumeration of k-local terms, aiming to compress the learning problem's effective dimension.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a definitive answer on the parameterized complexity of Hamiltonian learning, either delivering a significantly faster algorithm for high-order interactions or proving a conditional lower bound (e.g., under the Exponential Time Hypothesis or its variants) that justifies the polynomial barrier. It will thus chart the theoretical limits of efficient quantum system identification.
proposals.novelty: While recent work established algorithms for Hamiltonian learning, their complexity in the sparsity parameter k was not tightly characterized. This proposal systematically attacks this specific parameterized question by synthesizing tools from quantum Markov chains, learning from dynamics, and parameterized complexity theory, a novel combination for this problem.

proposals.title: Generative Modeling from Sparse Codes: An Inverse Problem via Hamiltonian Dynamics and Local Markov Structure
proposals.motivation: Sparse coding is a foundational model in machine learning for representing high-dimensional data, but the inverse problem—efficiently generating realistic data from a given sparse code—remains a significant challenge. This is crucial for creating robust synthetic datasets for algorithm testing and for understanding the generative implications of learned representations. Pre-2026, methods for learning sparse codes from data are mature, but principled, efficient generation from codes lacks a rigorous algorithmic framework, especially for complex, high-order dependencies.
proposals.hypothesis: That the structure of a sparse code—specifically, the conditional independence relations and locality constraints among its active components—can be leveraged to define a local, time-reversible Markov process (e.g., a quantum or classical Gibbs sampler) whose stationary distribution efficiently generates data consistent with the original code.
proposals.method: We will formalize the sparse code as a high-order Markov Random Field (MRF) over latent variables, building on recent work on learning MRFs from dynamics. The core algorithm will involve constructing a local Hamiltonian (or energy function) whose ground states correspond to valid data configurations for a given code. We will then study the real-time evolution under this Hamiltonian, as in Moitra's prior work on structure learning from evolution, to design a rapidly mixing Markov chain that samples from the target distribution. Techniques from the analysis of quantum Markov chains and the Dobrushin condition will be used to prove convergence rates.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first rigorous algorithmic framework for the inverse sparse coding problem with provable mixing times. It will bridge the gap between representation learning and generative modeling by showing how to turn a discriminatively learned sparse code into a efficient sampler. The results will also contribute new methods for designing local dynamics for sampling from complex, structured distributions.
proposals.novelty: The approach is non-obvious because it inverts the typical sparse coding pipeline by starting from the code structure to engineer dynamics, rather than learning from data. It uniquely combines tools from the analysis of quantum Markov chains, Hamiltonian learning, and high-order MRF learning—areas Moitra has recently worked in—to solve a core generative modeling problem.

proposals.title: Agnostic Learning of Local Hamiltonians and Markov Random Fields via Statistical Queries to Corrupted Dynamics
proposals.motivation: Agnostic learning of complex distributions, such as those defined by local Hamiltonians or higher-order Markov Random Fields (MRFs), is a fundamental challenge in machine learning and quantum many-body physics. Pre-2026, a significant barrier is the reliance on clean samples or specific distributional assumptions, which are unrealistic in experimental settings where data is corrupted by noise or adversarial perturbations. The Statistical Query (SQ) model provides a robust framework for such settings, but its application to agnostic learning of these structured models from dynamical data remains largely unexplored.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the dynamical evolution of a system—whether quantum (via Hamiltonian) or classical (via MRF dynamics)—encodes sufficient structural information to allow for agnostic parameter learning, even when access is limited to statistical queries on corrupted samples.
proposals.method: We will extend the techniques from Moitra's recent work on learning Hamiltonians from real-time evolution and learning MRFs from dynamics to the SQ framework. The core approach is to design a suite of statistical queries that probe correlation functions and conditional expectations from the noisy, corrupted data distribution. We will then develop a gradient-based or moment-matching algorithm within the SQ oracle model, leveraging the rapid mixing and conditional independence properties (e.g., Dobrushin conditions) established in Moitra's prior work to control query complexity and error propagation.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first agnostic learnability results for local Hamiltonians and higher-order MRFs in the robust SQ model, directly applicable to noisy quantum simulators and corrupted sensor networks. It will provide a rigorous algorithmic bridge between dynamical systems theory and robust machine learning, quantifying the trade-off between corruption tolerance, query complexity, and model accuracy.
proposals.novelty: The non-obvious element is that the dynamical structure of the model can be leveraged to design statistically efficient queries that bypass the need for clean samples, overcoming the 'noisy parity barrier' in an agnostic, corruption-tolerant setting, which was an open problem pre-2026.

proposals.title: Robust Learning of Quantum States and Hamiltonians via Filtered Dynamics and Improved Error Scaling
proposals.motivation: Robust learning algorithms, designed to tolerate a constant fraction of corrupted data, are crucial for quantum applications where state preparation and measurement are inherently noisy. However, pre-2026 results for learning distributions, quantum states, and Hamiltonians often suffer from error guarantees that scale linearly or nearly-linearly with the corruption fraction η, which is suboptimal compared to the √η scaling achievable in classical mean estimation. This barrier limits the practicality of quantum learning in adversarial or high-noise settings.
proposals.hypothesis: By leveraging the intrinsic dynamical structure of quantum systems—specifically, the evolution generated by local Hamiltonians—one can design robust filtering algorithms that achieve error dependence on the corruption fraction η that is better than linear, potentially approaching O(√η) or O(η^(2/3)), for learning quantum states and Hamiltonians.
proposals.method: We will extend the framework of learning from real-time evolution and quantum Markov chain mixing to the robust setting. The approach involves: 1) Using multiple short-time evolution snapshots to construct a robust moment estimation problem, treating corrupted samples as adversarial outliers. 2) Developing a quantum analogue of the filtering or stability techniques from classical robust statistics, potentially inspired by the preconditions for rapid mixing in quantum Markov chains (Dobrushin condition) to identify and downweight corrupted dynamical trajectories. 3) Applying this to learn the parameters of local Hamiltonians or to reconstruct quantum states, using the dynamical constraints to severely limit the adversary's ability to corrupt the learned information.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first robust learning algorithms for quantum systems with provably improved error scaling in the corruption fraction, directly applicable to quantum tomography and Hamiltonian learning. It will bridge the gap between classical robust statistics and quantum learning theory, establishing new connections via dynamical constraints.
proposals.novelty: The non-obvious element is using the physical constraints of Hamiltonian evolution—rather than just statistical properties of i.i.d. samples—as a powerful regularizer to filter corruptions, moving beyond direct translations of classical robust mean estimation algorithms.

proposals.title: Agnostic Learning of Arbitrary-Weight Mixtures of Two Product Distributions
proposals.motivation: Agnostically learning mixtures of product distributions is a fundamental problem in robust statistics and unsupervised learning. Prior work, such as that by Moitra and Valiant (2010), often relies on a balancedness condition (e.g., mixture weights bounded away from 0 or 1) to ensure identifiability and algorithmic tractability. This is a significant practical limitation, as real-world latent variable models may have highly imbalanced components.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by leveraging spectral methods on higher-order moment tensors and analyzing the dynamical properties of a derived statistical system, we can recover the parameters of a mixture of two product distributions without any assumption on the mixing weights, even in an agnostic (adversarial noise) setting.
proposals.method: We will formulate the parameter recovery problem as learning from the real-time evolution of a simple, derived Hamiltonian whose Gibbs state approximates the target mixture. Drawing on Moitra's recent work on structure learning of Hamiltonians from dynamics, we will develop an algorithm that uses carefully chosen local observables to construct and diagonalize a moment tensor from corrupted samples. The key technical step will be to show that the imbalance condition affects only the signal-to-noise ratio, not the algebraic identifiability, which can be overcome by analyzing the mixing time of the associated quantum Markov chain to bound sample complexity.
proposals.contribution: This work would provide the first efficient agnostic learning algorithm for two-product mixtures without balancedness assumptions, significantly expanding the applicability of mixture learning algorithms. It would also contribute new techniques linking classical robust statistics to the analysis of quantum dynamical systems for learning.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in bypassing the balancedness condition entirely, which is a standard and limiting assumption in the field, by using a Hamiltonian dynamics perspective and quantum Markov chain analysis to control recovery in the highly imbalanced regime.

proposals.title: Robust Learning of Quantum States and Hamiltonians from Corrupted Time-Series Data
proposals.motivation: Learning physical models from dynamical data is central to quantum simulation and verification. However, real-world experimental data from quantum devices is often corrupted by outliers and adversarial noise, violating standard assumptions of existing algorithms. Pre-2026 robust statistics provides powerful tools for mean estimation in high dimensions, but their application to complex quantum learning problems remains unexplored.
proposals.hypothesis: Robust mean estimation subroutines can be systematically integrated into algorithms for learning quantum states and Hamiltonians, enabling provable recovery guarantees even when a constant fraction of the time-series measurements are arbitrarily corrupted.
proposals.method: We will adapt recent robust mean estimation frameworks (e.g., filtering or stability-based algorithms) to function as subroutines within Moitra's established methods for learning from dynamics. Specifically, we will modify the structure learning algorithm for Hamiltonians from real-time evolution by replacing standard empirical average steps with robust counterparts to estimate expectation values from corrupted data. A similar approach will be applied to learning higher-order Markov Random Fields from dynamics, using robust estimation to handle noise in the inferred local conditional distributions.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first provably robust algorithms for learning quantum Hamiltonians and states from dynamical data, significantly advancing the theory of quantum learning with adversarial noise. It will bridge the fields of robust high-dimensional statistics and quantum learning, providing a new toolkit for reliable quantum system identification in noisy experimental settings.
proposals.novelty: While robust mean estimation is a classical tool, its application to quantum learning problems is non-trivial due to the complex, non-commutative structure of quantum observables and the specific forms of dynamical data. The novelty lies in the careful integration of these subroutines to preserve the convergence properties of the underlying quantum learning algorithms.

proposals.title: Streaming Algorithms for Agnostic Distribution Learning via Hamiltonian Dynamics and Spectral Methods
proposals.motivation: Agnostic distribution learning, where the target distribution may not belong to a known model class, is a fundamental challenge in robust statistics. The streaming setting, with a single pass over data, is critical for modern large-scale and time-sensitive applications. Pre-2026 work has largely treated these settings separately, leaving a gap in efficient, memory-bounded algorithms for agnostic learning from streams.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by leveraging spectral properties of data-generating processes and tools from Hamiltonian evolution analysis, one can design single-pass streaming algorithms that achieve near-optimal agnostic error guarantees with sublinear memory.
proposals.method: We will frame the stream of samples as a dynamical process and use techniques from Hamiltonian learning and quantum Markov chain analysis to construct a low-memory sketch of the data's spectral structure. Specifically, we will adapt methods from 'Structure Learning of Hamiltonians from Real-Time Evolution' to model classical streaming data as observations from an underlying dynamical system. This will allow us to apply Dobrushin-type conditions and rapid mixing analyses to control approximation error in the agnostic setting, bypassing the need to store the full dataset.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first rigorous framework for agnostic distribution learning in the single-pass streaming model with provable guarantees. It will bridge concepts from quantum dynamics and classical streaming algorithms, offering new tools for robust learning from sequential data.
proposals.novelty: The non-obvious synthesis lies in applying Hamiltonian evolution and high-temperature mixing analyses—developed for quantum systems—to the classical streaming agnostic learning problem, creating a novel spectral approach to memory-efficient density estimation.

proposals.title: Online Robust Dictionary Learning via Adversarial Dynamics and High-Temperature Markov Chains
proposals.motivation: Classical online dictionary learning algorithms assume sparse codes are drawn from a stationary, well-behaved distribution. This fails in real-time applications like adaptive sensing or quantum state tomography, where data streams can be non-stationary or contain adversarial outliers designed to corrupt the learned basis. Pre-2026, robust sparse recovery often relies on batch processing or assumes stochastic noise, leaving a gap for resilient, sequential learning in potentially hostile environments.
proposals.hypothesis: By formulating the online dictionary update as the evolution of a high-temperature quantum or classical Markov chain, we can design a dynamics-based learner that is provably robust to adversarial sparse codes and distribution shifts, achieving stable recovery even when a constant fraction of mini-batches are corrupted.
proposals.method: We will model the dictionary atoms as states of a system governed by a time-dependent Hamiltonian, where the data-driven update rule induces the evolution. Robustness will be enforced by integrating a Dobrushin-type condition from high-temperature Markov chains to ensure rapid mixing and stability against corrupt inputs. The algorithm will perform mini-batch gradient updates on a trimmed or Huber loss, with theoretical analysis leveraging conditional mutual information bounds to control error propagation from non-stationary or adversarial sparse codes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first online dictionary learning algorithm with provable adversarial robustness guarantees, bridging robust statistics and sequential learning. It will yield a practical tool for real-time signal processing in non-stationary settings and offer new theoretical connections between dynamical systems, Markov chain mixing, and robust optimization.
proposals.novelty: The fusion of adversarial training with a dynamics-based, Hamiltonian evolution framework for online learning is novel, as is the application of high-temperature Markov chain analytical tools (like Dobrushin conditions) to ensure robustness in incremental sparse coding.

proposals.title: Instance-Optimal Sparse Recovery via Adaptive Algorithm Selection and Hamiltonian-Inspired Diagnostics
proposals.motivation: Sparse recovery algorithms face a significant gap between worst-case theoretical guarantees and their excellent typical-case performance. This forces practitioners to be overly conservative in sparsity levels, wasting measurement resources. An instance-adaptive framework that certifies when a specific problem instance is 'easy' could unlock near information-theoretic limits in applications like compressive sensing and high-dimensional statistics.
proposals.hypothesis: That a low-complexity diagnostic phase, inspired by analyzing the dynamical properties of the measurement system, can reliably predict the success of a portfolio of recovery algorithms on a per-instance basis, enabling recovery at significantly higher sparsity levels for typical, non-adversarial inputs.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-phase framework. First, a diagnostic module will estimate instance-specific parameters (e.g., local coherence, restricted condition numbers, effective SNR) by analyzing the short-time evolution of a probe state under a Hamiltonian constructed from the measurement matrix, extending techniques from 'Structure Learning of Hamiltonians from Real-Time Evolution'. Second, a selector will map these features to a recovery algorithm (e.g., tuned variants of IHT, CoSaMP, or Lasso) and its parameters, using a learned policy trained on synthetic data from non-worst-case distributions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled, automated system for pushing sparse recovery to its empirical limits in practice, with high-probability instance-specific certificates. It will also contribute new theoretical tools connecting the dynamical properties of measurement systems to algorithmic performance.
proposals.novelty: The approach moves beyond worst-case analysis by integrating Hamiltonian dynamics as a diagnostic tool and formalizing instance-adaptive algorithm selection for sparse recovery, a departure from static algorithm design.

proposals.title: Minimax-Robust Learning of Hamiltonians and Markov Random Fields from Dynamics
proposals.motivation: Modern algorithms for learning physical models from dynamics, such as Hamiltonians or higher-order Markov Random Fields, rely on precise parametric assumptions. In practice, data is often generated by processes that violate these assumptions in complex, structured ways, leading to brittle performance. A principled framework for designing and certifying algorithms under non-parametric model uncertainty is urgently needed to bridge theory and real-world applications in quantum simulation and complex systems.
proposals.hypothesis: By formalizing model misspecification as a non-parametric uncertainty set and solving a minimax optimization problem, we can design learning algorithms with guaranteed worst-case performance certificates that remain effective even under significant, structured deviations from core stochastic assumptions.
proposals.method: We will develop a minimax framework where an inner adversarial loop, inspired by generative adversarial networks and robust optimization, perturbs the data-generating process within a Wasserstein or f-divergence ball around a nominal model (e.g., a high-temperature Gibbs state or a local Hamiltonian evolution). The outer loop will optimize algorithm parameters, leveraging Moitra's recent techniques for learning from dynamics and analyzing quantum Markov chains. Certificates will be derived via concentration of perturbed processes and stability bounds on the learned models.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a new class of certifiably robust algorithms for learning physical models from time-series data, with explicit performance guarantees under misspecification. It will establish a rigorous bridge between adversarial robustness in machine learning and the learning of structured distributions and quantum systems, providing practical tools for settings with imperfect models.
proposals.novelty: While robust statistics often handles specific contamination models, applying a non-parametric minimax formalism to the active design of algorithms for learning dynamical physical systems is novel. It combines adversarial robustness with the specific structure of Hamiltonian learning and MRF dynamics, moving beyond post-hoc analysis to proactive, certified design.

proposals.title: Robust Approximate Message Passing: State Evolution and Correction for Misspecified Models
proposals.motivation: Approximate Message Passing (AMP) provides sharp asymptotic guarantees for high-dimensional inference, but its celebrated state evolution and performance predictions rely critically on i.i.d. sub-Gaussian design matrices and well-specified likelihoods. In practice, data often violates these assumptions due to structured correlations, hidden confounders, or non-linear data-generating processes, leading AMP to produce unreliable estimates and invalid confidence intervals. A rigorous framework to understand and correct AMP's behavior under such misspecification is a major open problem at the intersection of statistical physics and theoretical machine learning.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the impact of common model misspecifications (e.g., feature correlations, latent variables, or non-linear generative models) on AMP can be precisely characterized as structured, non-Gaussian noise channels within its state evolution equations. This characterization will enable the design of corrective feedback terms and robust denoisers that restore the algorithm's asymptotic consistency and statistical validity.
proposals.method: We will develop a generalized AMP theory where the standard i.i.d. Gaussian assumption is replaced by a more general, possibly correlated or adversarially perturbed, random matrix model informed by the misspecification. Leveraging non-commutative operator techniques and dynamical functional analysis, akin to methods used for analyzing quantum Hamiltonians and Markov chains, we will derive the modified state evolution. This analysis will guide the synthesis of correction terms (akin to Onsager corrections) and the design of robust denoising functions, potentially using variational methods to estimate the effective noise channel from data.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a new AMP algorithm with provable robustness guarantees for a broad class of model violations, extending the practical applicability of AMP to real-world datasets. It will provide a theoretical toolkit for diagnosing inference failures due to misspecification and establish a novel connection between the statistical physics of inference and the problem of learning from corrupted or structured data.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has analyzed AMP under specific departures from ideal conditions, a unified framework that treats misspecification as a structured noise process within state evolution and actively corrects for it is novel. The approach uniquely blends tools from dynamical systems and statistical physics, areas Moitra has recently engaged with, to solve a core problem in robust statistics.

proposals.title: Robust Learning of Hamiltonians and Markov Random Fields from Dynamics under Model Misspecification
proposals.motivation: Recent breakthroughs in learning Hamiltonians and higher-order Markov Random Fields (MRFs) from dynamics, including Moitra's own work, assume data is generated exactly by the target model. Real-world quantum and classical systems are subject to unknown perturbations, external noise, and couplings beyond the assumed interaction structure. Understanding the robustness of these powerful learning algorithms to such model misspecification is crucial for their practical application in quantum simulation and systems biology.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that algorithms for learning from dynamics, such as those for Hamiltonian structure learning, possess an inherent robustness to certain structured perturbations (e.g., weak long-range couplings or local noise channels), and that this robustness can be formally quantified using Dobrushin-type conditions and information-theoretic stability arguments.
proposals.method: We will analyze Moitra's recent algorithms for learning Hamiltonians from real-time evolution and higher-order MRFs from dynamics. The technical approach will involve: 1) Introducing controlled misspecification (e.g., adding sparse, weak long-range terms to a local Hamiltonian or violating the Markov order assumption in the dynamics). 2) Deriving perturbation bounds on the learned parameters using stability lemmas for the system's generator (Liouvillian or classical transition matrix), extending the Dobrushin condition framework for quantum Markov chains. 3) Empirically validating the theoretical bounds on simulated quantum spin chains and classical lattice models.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first rigorous framework for robustness guarantees in learning from dynamical data, bridging the gap between idealized theory and practical implementation. It will yield concrete, testable conditions under which existing algorithms remain reliable, informing experimental design in quantum device characterization and complex system inference.
proposals.novelty: While model misspecification is studied in classical statistics, a systematic analysis for the novel class of algorithms that learn from *dynamics*—particularly quantum dynamics—is largely unexplored. The novelty lies in applying tools from the statistical mechanics of quantum Markov chains (e.g., Dobrushin conditions) to the problem of algorithmic robustness, a non-obvious connection.

proposals.title: Adaptive Data Pruning for Robust and Sample-Efficient Learning of High-Temperature Quantum Systems
proposals.motivation: Learning the parameters of quantum many-body Hamiltonians from time-evolution data is a central challenge in quantum simulation and tomography, but faces severe sample complexity barriers. Existing algorithms often assume perfect i.i.d. data or specific noise models, which are unrealistic in near-term quantum experiments where coherent noise, state preparation errors, and non-Markovian effects can violate these assumptions, leading to brittle performance.
proposals.hypothesis: By adaptively pruning or reweighting experimental data points based on a joint criterion of information gain (for rapid convergence under the model) and influence stability (for robustness to misspecification), we can design algorithms that simultaneously achieve lower sample complexity and graceful performance degradation under realistic experimental imperfections.
proposals.method: We will leverage Moitra's recent work on learning Hamiltonians from dynamics and rapid mixing in quantum Markov chains. The core technical approach is to develop a pruning score for each observed data point (e.g., a measurement outcome from a time-evolved state) that combines (1) its Fisher information for the Hamiltonian parameters, derived from the gradient of the log-likelihood under the learned model, with (2) its empirical influence function, measuring its impact on the parameter estimate under perturbed data distributions. We will formulate this as a convex optimization problem for sample weights, drawing on robust statistics and influence function theory, and integrate it into an iterative learning algorithm that alternates between parameter estimation and adaptive reweighting.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a theoretically grounded, polynomial-time algorithm for Hamiltonian learning with provable guarantees on both sample efficiency and robustness. It will bridge the gap between ideal-theory sample complexity and practical algorithmic stability, offering a new tool for quantum device characterization. The framework may also extend to learning classical higher-order Markov random fields from dynamics, where similar robustness issues arise.
proposals.novelty: While data pruning for efficiency (e.g., coresets) and for robustness (e.g., trimming) exist separately, their unification via a principled, adaptive criterion tailored to dynamical system learning is novel. Applying this fusion to the quantum learning setting, where the data comes from physical time evolution and misspecification is structural, is a significant departure from prior work.

proposals.title: Improved Sample Complexity for Learning Local Hamiltonians from Real-Time Evolution via Spectral Sparsification and Adaptive Measurements
proposals.motivation: Recent work by Moitra and others has established polynomial-time algorithms for learning the structure and parameters of local Hamiltonians from observations of their real-time evolution. However, the sample complexity and runtime constants of these algorithms, while polynomial, remain prohibitively high for practical implementation on near-term quantum simulators or devices. Reducing these costs is essential for applying these powerful learning frameworks to real experimental data in quantum many-body physics and quantum chemistry.
proposals.hypothesis: By integrating techniques from randomized numerical linear algebra, specifically Hamiltonian sparsification, and by designing adaptive measurement strategies that leverage the learned structure iteratively, we can significantly reduce the sample and time complexity constants of Hamiltonian learning algorithms while preserving theoretical guarantees.
proposals.method: We will develop an algorithm that first uses a small, randomized set of evolution timepoints and local observables to construct a coarse, spectrally sparsified approximation of the unknown Hamiltonian. This initial model will guide an adaptive measurement phase, where subsequent time evolution probes are chosen to maximize information gain about the largest remaining uncertain couplings, akin to techniques in active learning. The core technical tool will be a robust variant of the Hessian-based learning framework from 'Structure Learning of Hamiltonians from Real-Time Evolution', accelerated using fast Johnson-Lindenstrauss transforms for dimension reduction in the parameter space.
proposals.contribution: This work will yield a concretely more efficient, implementable algorithm for Hamiltonian learning, directly lowering the experimental burden for characterizing quantum systems. It will bridge the gap between the theoretical computer science of quantum learning and the practical constraints of quantum simulation platforms. The integration of sparsification and adaptive design will provide a blueprint for efficiency improvements in other quantum learning tasks.
proposals.novelty: While sparsification is classic in graph algorithms and adaptive measurements are known in classical statistics, their synergistic application to reduce the practical cost of *quantum* Hamiltonian learning is novel. This approach moves beyond analyzing the asymptotic order of the polynomial to aggressively minimizing the leading constants, a non-trivial optimization given the noisy, constrained nature of quantum observations.

proposals.title: Provably Robust Dictionary Learning via Tensor Initialization and Constrained Hamiltonian Dynamics
proposals.motivation: Dictionary learning is a fundamental problem in machine learning, but standard methods are sensitive to noise and initialization, often converging to poor local minima. Recent work by Moitra and others on learning Hamiltonians from real-time evolution and on tensor methods for higher-order statistics provides a new theoretical lens. This project aims to bridge these areas to create a robust, provably correct learning algorithm for adversarial or noisy data settings.
proposals.hypothesis: A hybrid framework that initializes a dictionary via a robust tensor decomposition of higher-order moments and then refines it through a constrained optimization derived from a Hamiltonian dynamical model will yield dictionaries with both strong identifiability guarantees and practical robustness to data imperfections.
proposals.method: We will first apply a robust tensor power method or Jennrich's algorithm to third-order moment tensors of the data to obtain a stable initial dictionary estimate. This initialization will then be used in a constrained optimization loop, formulated as a sparse Bayesian learning problem or a penalized maximum likelihood, where the constraints are informed by the dynamical structure of the data-generating process, akin to learning from Hamiltonian evolution. We will analyze the mixing time and convergence of this two-stage process using tools from quantum Markov chains and high-temperature regimes to ensure stability.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new algorithm with provable convergence and robustness guarantees for dictionary learning under model misfit and noise. It will theoretically unify moment-based identifiability with model-based optimization, offering a path to bypass the noisy parity barrier in learning higher-order structures. The analysis will contribute new techniques for understanding the landscape of constrained learning problems.
proposals.novelty: The non-obvious synthesis lies in using robust tensor initialization—a tool for learning latent variable models—to seed a Hamiltonian-inspired constrained optimization for dictionary learning, explicitly analyzing its dynamics through the lens of quantum Markov chain mixing.

proposals.title: Algebraic Dictionary Learning via Tensor Decomposition of Higher-Order Moments
proposals.motivation: Dictionary learning is a fundamental problem in signal processing and machine learning, but standard iterative algorithms like K-SVD can be slow and sensitive to initialization. Recent work in quantum state tomography, particularly by Ankur Moitra and others, has shown that the CP decomposition of a moment tensor can directly and efficiently recover the pure states composing a quantum system. This suggests a powerful algebraic framework for latent variable models that remains underexplored in classical learning contexts.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the dictionary learning problem, under a sparse linear model with non-Gaussian independent coefficients, can be reformulated as a tensor decomposition problem on empirical higher-order moment tensors, enabling exact, non-iterative recovery of the dictionary atoms with provable guarantees.
proposals.method: We will construct a third- or fourth-order moment tensor from observed signal patches, analogous to the quantum tomography setting. Leveraging pre-2026 tensor decomposition algorithms like Jennrich's algorithm or robust power method variants, we will directly extract the dictionary columns as the rank-1 components of the CP decomposition. The core technical work involves establishing the precise statistical conditions (e.g., sparsity, coefficient distribution) under which the constructed tensor's CP decomposition is unique and stable, adapting the analysis from quantum information theory to the classical signal model.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a provably correct, non-iterative algorithm for dictionary learning with strong identifiability guarantees, potentially offering exponential speed-ups over iterative methods. It will forge a concrete theoretical and algorithmic bridge between moment-based methods in quantum tomography and classical unsupervised learning, enriching both fields.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct adaptation of the CP tensor decomposition framework from quantum state tomography—a technique developed for recovering quantum Hamiltonians and states—to the classical dictionary learning problem, providing an algebraic, initialization-free solution where only iterative, non-convex optimization methods were previously standard.

proposals.title: Cavity Belief Propagation for Sparse Coding: A Statistical Physics Approach to Approximate Inference
proposals.motivation: Sparse coding is a fundamental problem in machine learning and signal processing, but exact inference is intractable. Pre-2026, approximate inference methods like variational Bayes or MAP estimation are standard, yet they can struggle with complex posteriors and convergence in high-dimensional, underdetermined regimes. A principled, physics-inspired framework could offer new algorithmic insights and improved performance guarantees.
proposals.hypothesis: The cavity method from statistical physics, which provides exact solutions on tree-like factor graphs and powerful approximations for loopy graphs, can be rigorously adapted to derive a novel, convergent message-passing algorithm for sparse coding that offers superior characterization of the solution landscape compared to standard variational methods.
proposals.method: We will formulate the sparse coding model with a Laplace or spike-and-slab prior as a factor graph.
